pattern: Because the cost of your next move depends only on how many moves you've made so far and alternates between two fixed values, tracking a room together with the parity of the move count turns the alternating-cost rule into an ordinary shortest-path search over a doubled state space.
hints: The cost of your very next move only depends on whether you've made an even or odd number of moves so far, alternating 1, 2, 1, 2, ... starting with 1 -- so track that parity as part of your search state alongside the room itself.

From a (room, parity) state at time t, moving into a neighboring room costs whichever of 1 or 2 the current parity dictates, but you still cannot start that move until the neighbor's opening time has passed -- your arrival time is the later of your current time or the room's opening time, plus that move's cost.
Run a shortest-path search over this doubled state space (room, parity), and the final answer is the smaller of the two arrival times at the bottom-right room across both possible parities.

edge_inputs.description: small grid where waiting is required before the first move
every room already open, pure alternating travel time
all rooms share the same large opening time
the direct path is blocked by a late-opening room, forcing a detour whose move-parity differs from the direct route
the start room itself has a nonzero opening time, which never matters since you begin there at time zero
opening times at the upper bound

stress.description: random grids with random opening times
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 40)))
    m = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 40)))
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 1000000000) for _ in range(m)] for _ in range(n)]
    grid[0][0] = 0
    return [grid]

complexity.time: O(n*m*log(n*m)) with a priority queue over the doubled state space (the authored JS reference uses a simpler O((n*m)^2) linear scan)
